original_abstract: In lithium metal anodes (LMAs) operating at >95% Coulombic efficiency, the formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) represent the primary drivers of capacity loss. Despite its importance, the fundamental mechanisms governing this degradation remain elusive. A critical factor influencing SEI evolution is its inherent solubility within the electrolyte. In this study, we utilize *in operando* electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM) to systematically quantify and compare the solubility of SEIs formed in LMA-optimized ether-based electrolytes. Our findings establish a clear correlation between SEI solubility, passivity, and long-term cyclability, demonstrating that dissolution is a dominant factor in the performance variations observed across different battery chemistries. By integrating EQCM data with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis, we reveal that SEI stability is a function of both interphase composition and the chemical properties of the bulk electrolyte. This work provides essential insights for designing more stable interfaces, offering a strategic pathway to minimize capacity loss during the cycling and aging of high-performance batteries.
humanized_abstract: Lithium metal anodes (LMAs) have attracted increasing attention as a new replacement for traditional graphite anodes due to their high capacities and excellent rate performance. To minimize the loss in capacity caused by the long cycle life and high utilization of LMAs, the lithium metal anodes generally operate at more than 95% Coulombic efficiency. As a result, the formation and thickness increase of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) are the main factors which lead to capacity loss. Unfortunately, the degradation mechanism of the SEI is still unclear. In the present work, the influence of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) solubility, which has rarely been reported, on the electrochemical performance of lithium metal anodes is investigated by exploring the composition, passivity and cycle stability in LMA-optimized ether-based electrolytes using *in operando* electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments. Our results clearly show that the cycle stability is affected by the solubility of the SEI which strongly correlates with the passivity and cycle stability for different battery systems. This paper therefore explores the correlations among the solubility of SEI, electrode passivity and cycle stability. Understanding the factors of SEI stability will help to modulate the content of SEI components and influence the electrolyte environment so as to achieve the desired properties of batteries to minimize capacity loss during the cycling and long-term aging process.
